days.label: Day 1
days.title: The First Class
days.subtitle: Alex walks into a packed lecture hall and tries not to look lost.

days.label: Day 2
days.title: The Bad Grade
days.subtitle: The first quiz comes back rough, and the spiral starts fast.

days.label: Day 3
days.title: The Money Problem
days.subtitle: Textbook prices hit harder than Alex expected.

days.label: Day 4
days.title: The Breakdown
days.subtitle: Midterms are close, sleep is bad, and everything feels louder.

days.label: Day 5
days.title: The Turnaround
days.subtitle: What changed this week was not the pressure. It was whether Alex knew where to go.

badges.title: DARS Navigator
badges.description: Alex learned that DARS is a degree progress map, not a mystery acronym you are supposed to already know.

badges.title: Tutoring Explorer
badges.description: Alex saw what tutoring actually looks like: normal room, normal students, normal questions.

badges.title: Office Hours Pro
badges.description: Alex learned that emailing a professor does not need perfect wording to work.

badges.title: Financial Coach Found
badges.description: Alex discovered that money help can be practical, private, and not just for emergencies.

badges.title: Scholarship Hunter
badges.description: Alex stopped assuming no and checked what scholarships might actually fit.

badges.title: Wellness Warrior
badges.description: Alex learned counseling is not only for crisis. It can just be a place to talk before things get worse.

endings.title: Alex Thrives
endings.summary: Alex still has hard days. But now Alex knows where to go. That's the difference.

endings.title: Alex Survives
endings.summary: Alex is getting there. Some doors are still closed. But at least Alex knows they exist.

endings.title: Alex Struggles
endings.summary: Alex is doing it all alone. It's harder than it has to be. But it's not too late.

jumpIns.title: Tutoring Jump-In
jumpIns.recap: Alex just got a 52 on the first quiz and is choosing support over silence.

jumpIns.title: Advising Jump-In
jumpIns.recap: Alex is trying to decode degree planning language before it becomes a bigger problem.

jumpIns.title: First-Day Jump-In
jumpIns.recap: Alex has barely found a seat in the lecture hall. Everything is new and moving too fast.

scenes.title: The lecture hall feels enormous.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex slips into a seat near the back. Three hundred students, a syllabus full of links, and a professor already talking like everyone knows the system.

scenes.title: The professor mentions DARS for degree planning.
scenes.speaker: Professor
scenes.body: Office hours. DARS. Tutoring on the syllabus. Alex catches the words, but not what any of them are supposed to mean.

choices.label: "What's DARS? Let me look it up."
choices.caption: Take the small uncomfortable step.

scenes.title: Later that night...
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex stares at the class planner and still does not know which boxes matter. Nothing exploded. It just stayed unclear.

scenes.title: DARS is basically a degree progress map.
scenes.speaker: Alex
scenes.body: The acronym stops being intimidating once Alex sees what it actually does: show what counts, what is missing, and what needs a closer look.

cards.title: What it is
cards.body: DARS helps students see completed requirements, in-progress classes, and what still needs attention.

cards.title: Why it matters
cards.body: Alex does not need to memorize every rule on day one. DARS gives the shape of the degree in one place.

cards.title: What happens next
cards.body: If something looks off, that is when advising becomes useful instead of mysterious.

bullets: Open DARS from MyASU when schedule planning feels fuzzy.
Use it to spot missing requirements before registration gets chaotic.
Pair it with advising when the report raises more questions than it answers.

scenes.title: A classmate mentions a study group.
scenes.speaker: Classmate
scenes.body: "A few of us are meeting later if you want in." Alex has known these people for maybe nine minutes.

scenes.title: Alex heads back alone.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Nothing dramatic happens. Alex just keeps carrying all the uncertainty without seeing what classmates are also confused about.

scenes.title: The group is less awkward than expected.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Nobody has everything figured out. That alone makes the campus feel a little less like a place Alex has to decode in secret.

scenes.title: The quiz comes back with a 52.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex feels the heat rush in fast. It is the kind of grade that makes every fear sound smarter than every plan.

scenes.title: What does Alex do?
scenes.speaker: Alex
scenes.body: The campus suddenly feels full of people who probably understand college better than Alex does.

choices.label: "I'm just dumb. I'll try harder next time."
choices.caption: Keep the panic private.

scenes.title: Three weeks later...
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex keeps trying alone, but the grade pattern does not change. Working harder without new help mostly means feeling worse in a quieter room.

scenes.title: Tutoring feels a lot less scary once Alex gets there.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex books a session, walks in, and finds a regular study room with a tutor and a few other students. Nobody expects a polished explanation.

cards.title: Booking it
cards.body: Alex uses the tutoring site to find the course, the time, and the format that fits.

cards.title: Walking in
cards.body: The room looks like school, not like a tribunal. People are working, asking questions, and comparing notes.

cards.title: What the tutor does
cards.body: The tutor asks what is confusing, looks at the quiz and homework, and helps Alex break the problem down.

bullets: Bring notes, homework, or even just the problem set that is not clicking.
It is normal if the first sentence is, 'I do not know where I am stuck.'
Lots of students use tutoring before they feel ready, not after.

scenes.title: The email does not need to sound perfect.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex writes a simple, honest note asking about the quiz and office hours. The professor replies like this question is normal, because it is.

emailMock.subject: Question about quiz 1 and office hours
emailMock.body: Hi Professor, I scored lower than I expected on Quiz 1 and I want to improve before the next one. Could I come to office hours to ask where I went wrong? Thank you, Alex.
emailMock.replySubject: Re: Question about quiz 1 and office hours
emailMock.replyBody: Hi Alex, thanks for reaching out. Yes, come by office hours tomorrow and bring your quiz. We can talk through what tripped you up and how to prepare for the next one.

bullets: Office hours are for confusion, not just emergencies.
Professors expect students to need clarification.

scenes.title: The textbook bill lands badly.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex adds up books, lab fees, and everything that felt small until it stacked. The math starts feeling personal.

scenes.title: Alex hears about financial coaching.
scenes.speaker: Friend from the hall
scenes.body: "It is not weird," they say. "They literally help you make sense of the money stuff."

choices.label: "That's probably not for me."
choices.caption: Shrink the option before trying it.

choices.label: "I'll check it out."
choices.caption: Give the resource a real look.

scenes.title: The stress stays vague and heavy.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex does what a lot of students do: keeps worrying in private and hopes the numbers somehow get gentler on their own.

scenes.title: Money help can be practical, not dramatic.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The explainer makes it feel normal: a confidential conversation about budgeting, aid steps, deadlines, and what to do before the stress snowballs.

cards.title: What it is
cards.body: A place to talk through expenses, timelines, and options without needing to already understand financial aid language.

cards.title: What happens in a session
cards.body: Alex would walk through the specific pressure points: books, aid timing, bills, and what questions to ask next.

cards.title: What it is not
cards.body: It is not a judgment session and it is not only for crisis. It is just money clarity.

bullets: Free guidance is still worth using, even if the problem feels 'not serious enough.'
Talking early usually creates more options than waiting.
Money stress counts as school stress.

scenes.title: A scholarship flyer catches Alex's eye.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The default thought arrives instantly: this is probably for someone more prepared, more impressive, more anything.

scenes.title: Alex walks past it.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The game reveals something Alex never gets to see in the moment: three scholarships actually matched. They stay invisible because Alex never checked.

scenes.title: Checking is better than assuming no.
scenes.speaker: Alex
scenes.body: Alex opens the checker instead of self-rejecting. Even finding only a few matches feels better than letting guesswork decide.

cards.title: Why this matters
cards.body: Scholarship opportunities often hide behind filters, deadlines, and confidence gaps more than qualifications.

cards.title: What Alex learns
cards.body: Eligibility is often broader than the inner critic says it is.

scenes.title: The week catches up all at once.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Midterms are coming. Alex is not sleeping well and has quietly started thinking about dropping a class just to breathe.

scenes.title: Alex's roommate mentions counseling services.
scenes.speaker: Roommate
scenes.body: "You don't have to be in crisis," they say. "Sometimes it just helps to talk to someone who knows college stress is real."

scenes.title: Alex closes the laptop.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Nothing about the overwhelm gets smaller. Alex just has one less option in view while still carrying all of it.

scenes.title: Counseling does not have to be a last resort.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex learns that a first visit can just be a conversation about stress, sleep, overwhelm, or feeling off. It is not only for emergencies.

cards.title: The first conversation
cards.body: Mostly questions: what has been feeling hard, what has changed, and what kind of support sounds useful.

cards.title: What Alex does not need
cards.body: A perfect explanation, a crisis script, or permission to feel overwhelmed.

cards.title: Why it helps
cards.body: Naming the stress out loud can make it feel less like a private flaw and more like something support can actually touch.

bullets: Looking into counseling is not the same as committing to something scary.
It can just be a first conversation.
Students use it for school pressure, not only severe emergencies.

scenes.title: An advisor email comes in.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: It is the kind of message Alex might normally ignore because it sounds official and maybe disappointing.

choices.label: Open it.
choices.caption: See what the nudge actually says.

scenes.title: The unread dot stays there.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: Alex avoids one more thing that might have helped. It does not feel like a big decision. Those are often the ones that quietly add up.

scenes.title: The nudge is warmer than Alex expected.
scenes.speaker: Advisor email
scenes.body: Instead of sounding punitive, the email sounds normal and specific. It points to tutoring without making Alex feel like a problem to be solved.

messageMock.title: A quick note before next week
messageMock.body: Hi Alex. A lot of students in your class use tutoring early, not just after things get bad. If the first quiz felt rough, here is what tutoring actually looks like and how to get started. No pressure.

bullets: Warm nudges work better than scary warnings.
The goal is to normalize help, not shame students into it.
Opening one email can reopen several options.

scenes.title: The pressure is still real. The map is what changed.
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: College did not suddenly get easy. But depending on the week Alex just had, the campus now feels either slightly more navigable or much lonelier.

scenes.title: End of week one
scenes.speaker: Narrator
scenes.body: The ending depends on what Alex chose to try, avoid, and trust during the week.
